Some say bandits, some say the mines ran dry. Either way, stock up.
The traders won't talk about it. That alone tells you something.
Prices like these don't happen without a reason.
Last caravan that came through had half its wagons empty. Driver said he couldn't find sellers.
Iron's worth double what it was a month ago. Whoever's hoarding it has deep pockets.
The merchant guild rerouted everything through the northern pass. Longer road, higher cost, fewer shipments.
I've been doing this long enough to know when someone's buying up stock to corner a market.
There's good coin to be made running supplies east right now. If you don't mind the risk.
The dye merchants packed up and left two weeks ago. Didn't sell their remaining stock, just left.
Grain prices are steady here, but one settlement over they're paying triple. Something's cutting off their supply.
A buyer came through offering gold for medicinal herbs. Whole cartloads. Wouldn't say who they were for.
Nobody's seen a shipment of timber in weeks. The mill upstream is either closed or selling to someone else.
You can tell a lot from what people stop buying. Nobody's buying seed grain. They don't expect to plant.
The smiths are melting down old tools for the metal. That's not frugality, that's desperation.
Word is the coastal routes are closed. Salt and fish will get expensive soon.
Someone's paying mercenaries to escort wagons on a road that used to be perfectly safe.
Every time a new trader shows up, they're charging more and carrying less. The math isn't hard.
The jeweler left town overnight. Took everything. When they run, the trouble's already close.
Leather's cheap because the herds are being culled. People are scared to keep livestock out in the open.
Barter's coming back. When people stop trusting coin, that tells you how they feel about the future.
